text: The endoscopic view reveals a massive diverticulum in the distal esophagus, characterized by a wide-based opening.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic resection of the epiphrenic diverticulum, utilizing robotic assistance to carefully excise the diverticulum from the distal esophagus.
The surgeon ligates the short gastric vessels to free the greater curvature of the stomach, then proceeds to the gastroesophageal junction to identify the left crura.
The surgeon identifies the right crura and completes the posterior dissection, revealing the epiphrenic diverticulum in the surgical field.

The surgeon continues to dissect the esophagus proximally, carefully separating the diverticulum from its pleural attachments using the alveolar tissue planes in the mediastinum. The dissection progresses until the proximal edge of the diverticulum is identified, allowing it to be completely reduced out of the chest.

The surgeon mobilizes the epiphrenic diverticulum from the mediastinum and divides it using two fires of a blue load stapler with reinforced staple lines. A 44 French bougie is placed to prevent esophageal narrowing during division. The staple line is inspected, and the diverticulum specimen is removed from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a robotic Heller myotomy, using a hook cautery to carefully clear surrounding tissue from the esophagus, ensuring the esophageal wall is clearly visualized.

The surgeon scores the anterior surface of the esophageal wall before initiating the myotomy. The circular muscle layer is identified and divided, working proximally towards the diverticulum while carefully preserving the anterior vagus nerve as it crosses the esophagus. The myotomy extends onto the stomach for two to three centimeters, reaching the edge of the diverticulum, with the vagus nerve visibly preserved.
The surgeon performs laparoscopic curl closure using an endoscopic suturing device with barbed proline suture, followed by a toupee fundoplication.

The surgeon wraps the fundus posterior to the esophagus to initiate the fundoplication. The stomach is grasped near the short gastric vessels, and the shoe shine maneuver is performed to prevent twisting of the fundus, ensuring sufficient stomach for a loose fundoplication. A suturing device is used to complete the toupee fundoplication, which is then displayed in its final form.

The completion endoscopy reveals the removal of the diverticulum, with an intact staple line and a wide open gastroesophageal junction. The postoperative upper GI imaging is displayed, confirming the surgical outcome.
